title: Peshwari naan
subtitle: Sweet filled flatbread with almonds and coconut
excerpt: Plain naan dough stuffed with a sweet filling of desiccated coconut, ground almonds, and a touch of sugar, then sealed and cooked in a hot dry pan. The filling caramelises slightly against the hot pan surface, and the naan is finished with melted butter and a scatter of flaked almonds. The sweetest bread on the British Indian menu.
sourceNotes: Peshwari naan (also Peshawari, named after Peshawar in present-day Pakistan) is a sweet filled naan that became popular on British Indian restaurant menus in the 1980s. The filling of coconut and almonds is a British-Indian adaptation rather than a traditional Pashtun bread.

content.text: The dough for peshwari naan is the same plain naan dough. The difference is in the filling: a sweet paste of desiccated coconut, ground almonds, and sugar that goes inside the dough before rolling. The filling must be well distributed in a thin, even layer, too much filling in one spot will tear through the dough when rolled.

The technique for filling naan is simpler than it appears: flatten a dough ball, place the filling in the centre, gather the edges up and over, pinch to seal, then roll gently. The pinched seal should be underneath when rolling, so any seam failure is on the top (less visible) surface. Don't be tempted to overfill, a thin layer throughout is better than a thick pocket in the centre.

Mix the {{plain-flour}}, {{fast-action-yeast}}, {{sea-salt-fine}}, and 1 teaspoon {{caster-sugar}} in a large bowl. Add the {{plain-yoghurt}}, {{milk}}, and {{vegetable-oil}} and mix to a soft dough. Knead for 5 minutes. Place in an oiled bowl, cover, and rest for 1 hour. Mix the {{desiccated-coconut}}, {{almond-flour}}, and 2 tablespoons {{caster-sugar}} together to make the filling.

Divide the dough into 4 pieces. Flatten each piece in your palm to about 10 cm across. Place 2 heaped tablespoons of filling in the centre. Bring the edges up and over the filling, pinch to seal completely, and turn seam-side down. Roll gently on a floured surface into an oval about 20 cm long, taking care the filling doesn't burst through. Heat a dry pan over maximum heat until smoking. Cook for 60 to 90 seconds per side until blistered and char-spotted. Brush immediately with {{unsalted-butter}} and scatter with {{flaked-almonds}} if using.

items.fix: Use no more than 2 tablespoons of filling per naan. Pinch the seal completely before rolling, and roll gently outward from the centre rather than pressing down hard
items.cause: Too much filling or not sealed properly
items.symptom: Filling bursts through the dough during rolling

items.fix: The dry coconut-almond filling is less prone to leaking than moist fillings. If using raisins in the filling, reduce the amount to keep the mixture dry
items.cause: Filling too wet or seal opened
items.symptom: Filling leaks out and burns in the pan

items.fix: Taste the filling before use. It should be noticeably sweet. If using unsweetened coconut, increase the sugar slightly
items.cause: Sugar insufficient or desiccated coconut unsweetened

items.fix: Peshwari naans are slightly thicker than plain naan because of the filling, aim for 5 to 6 mm before filling, slightly thinner after rolling with filling inside
items.cause: Rolled too thin or dough not rested enough
items.symptom: Naan doesn't puff with filling inside

content.text: Peshwari naan has a clear place on a British curry-house menu. It is the sweet bread. People order it when they want a contrast to the savoury mains, not just a plain side. The sweetness works alongside mild curries like korma, passanda, and butter chicken. It also balances a table where the main dishes lean hot. The scattering of flaked almonds on the surface has become a visual shorthand for the dish across British Indian menus.
